topic: Someone finds a time capsule they don't remember burying
raw_output:
[story 1]

The dust motes danced in the single shaft of sunlight piercing the attic gloom, illuminating the tarnished metal box. Liam shoved aside a stack of yellowed newspapers, his movements jerky and impatient. He hadn't meant to be up here, not really. The whole house felt like a trap, suffocatingly still. Then he saw it—a heavy, sealed container tucked beneath a moth-eaten tapestry. His fingers, usually steady, trembled slightly as he lifted it. The latch was corroded, protesting with a sharp, metallic groan. Inside, nestled in brittle, faded velvet, were photographs and letters. A sudden, cold knot tightened in his stomach. Who buried this? He felt a surge of irritation, a deep-seated refusal to be burdened by forgotten things. He flipped through the contents, his jaw tightening as he read a childish drawing. "This isn't mine," he muttered, the sound rough in the silence. He slammed the lid shut, the click echoing like a final judgment. He didn't want this.

[story 2]

Sarah traced the outline of the time capsule with a fingertip, her breath catching in short, sharp intakes. The air in the basement was thick with the smell of damp earth and forgotten things. She had been tasked with clearing out the crawlspace, a chore she loathed. When she unearthed the heavy, cylindrical object, a distinct tension coiled in her shoulders. It was clearly sealed, a relic from some distant past. Her gaze swept over the contents—a brittle diary, a faded ticket stub. A wave of profound annoyance washed over her. Why would someone leave this behind? It felt like an imposition, an unwelcome obligation thrust upon her. She pulled back quickly, her eyes flashing with a kind of sharp defensiveness. "Just throw it out," she snapped at the empty room, her voice tight, unwilling to engage with the mystery. She needed to get back to the surface, to anything that felt real and manageable.

[story 3]

Mark found the box wedged behind the old furnace. He didn't look pleased; he looked annoyed, his brow furrowed deeply as he wrestled the heavy container free. The weight of it seemed disproportionate to its size, demanding attention he didn't wish to give. When he finally pried the lid open, the faint scent of aged paper hit him, instantly grating on his nerves. Inside lay relics of a life he hadn't lived, a collection of items from decades ago. A simmering resentment began to build in his chest. This intrusion felt deliberate, an unwelcome spotlight on some unknown history. He didn't want to connect with it. With a frustrated grunt, Mark shoved the capsule back into its hiding spot, wiping a bead of sweat from his temple. He turned sharply, eager to escape the suffocating presence of the discovery.
